1: mizbeach (miz-BE-akh): 'altar' (H4196, from zabach H2076 — to slaughter/sacrifice). The altar is named from the act it enables: the slaughter of sacrifice. It is 5 cubits × 5 cubits × 3 cubits high — square, with equal sides. The square shape may symbolize completeness and equal access from all four directions. The altar of burnt offering stands at the entrance to the tabernacle courtyard — the first thing encountered when approaching the divine dwelling. There is no access to the sanctuary without first passing the altar of sacrifice.
2: qarayyim (kar-NAI-yim): 'horns' (H7161, from qeren — horn, also power/authority H7161). The four horns project from the four corners of the altar — one piece with the altar, not added on. The horns are where the blood of the sin offering is applied (Leviticus 4:7, 18), and where fugitives clung for asylum (1 Kings 1:50; Exodus 2:28). The horn is simultaneously the place of atonement and the place of refuge — power channeled into protection. nechoshet overlay: bronze, the material of the outer court.
3: The five altar utensils: sirotav (pots/buckets for removing ash H5518); ya'av (shovels H3257); mizraqotav (sprinkling bowls H4219 — for receiving and dashing the blood against the altar sides); mizlegotav (flesh hooks H4207 — for handling the sacrificial portions); machtotav (fire pans H4289 — for carrying coals). Every aspect of sacrificial service has its designated tool. The whole cultic system is equipped and ordered — nothing left to improvisation at the place of sacrifice.
4: mikbar nechoshet (mik-BAR ne-KHO-shet): 'bronze grating/lattice' (H4345 H5178). A bronze network or mesh is made for the altar — a latticework platform beneath the sacrificial fire. Bronze withstands heat (unlike gold or silver); the grating allows ash to fall while supporting the burning sacrifice above. The technical sophistication of this design — a suspended combustion platform — reflects that the revealed pattern encompasses engineering as well as theology.
5: The bronze grating is placed under the karkov (kar-KOV): 'ledge/rim/shelf' (H3764 — a projecting shelf around the altar exterior). The grating is suspended at the midpoint of the altar — not at the top or bottom but in the middle. This arrangement creates a lower chamber (for ash collection) and an upper chamber (for the fire and sacrifice), with the grating as the dividing floor between them.
6: Acacia wood poles (baddei shittim) overlaid with bronze for carrying the altar. The altar travels the same way as the ark and table — by poles through rings. But the altar's poles are bronze-clad (not gold), matching the outer court material system. Every object has poles; every sacred object is portable. The God of the desert tabernacle does not dwell in a fixed temple but travels with his pilgrim people.
7: The poles are inserted through the rings on the sides of the altar for carrying. The spatial arrangement ensures that the altar can be lifted and carried without the bearers touching the sacrificial surface. The altar is carried by priests — the Kohathites are assigned the altar in Numbers 4:13-14. What has received the blood of sacrifice requires careful handling in transport.
8: nevuv luchot taa'aseh oto (ne-VOOV loo-KHOT ta-ah-SEH oh-TO): 'hollow with boards you shall make it' — the altar is hollow, constructed of bronze-overlaid boards, not solid. ka'asher her'ah otkha bahar ken ya'asu: 'as he showed you on the mountain, so they shall make it.' The tabnit principle again — the altar design, like every other element, is revealed, not invented. The hollow construction makes it portable (lighter than solid stone or metal) while maintaining the sacred form revealed on the mountain.
9: chatzer ha-mishkan (khatz-AR hah-mish-KAN): 'the courtyard of the tabernacle' (H2691, from chatzar — to enclose/surround; also a village settlement in the wilderness). The courtyard is the outermost sacred zone — still sacred, but open to all Israel (not just priests). The south side is 100 cubits long — fine twisted linen hangings (qelai shesh moshzar) creating a white linen enclosure that marks off the sacred precincts from the camp.
10: Twenty pillars and twenty bronze sockets for the south side — one socket per pillar. vaveihem (vah-VEI-hem): 'their hooks' (H2053 — the letter vav itself means hook/nail, shaped like a hook). chashuqeihem (kha-shoo-KEI-hem): 'their bands/connecting rods' (H2838). Silver hooks and bands on the bronze-socketed pillars — silver connects the courtyard pillars to each other, creating a unified white linen enclosure. The courtyard is bounded by silver at the top of its pillars, bronze at the base.
11: The north side exactly mirrors the south: 100 cubits of linen hangings, twenty bronze-socketed pillars, silver hooks and bands. The bilateral symmetry of the courtyard (north = south) reflects the divine orderliness that pervades the entire tabernacle design. What is said of one wall is equally true of the other — no hierarchy between the lateral approaches to the divine dwelling.
12: The west end of the courtyard: 50 cubits wide, ten pillars with ten sockets. The courtyard is 100 cubits (east-west) by 50 cubits (north-south) — twice as long as it is wide. The proportional rectangle (Exodus 2:1) mirrors the inner tabernacle structure, creating nested rectangular spaces of increasing holiness: outer courtyard (100×50), tabernacle itself (30×10), with the veil dividing the tabernacle into holy place (20×10) and holy of holies (10×10).
13: The east side (miqdma qedmah — toward the east) is also 50 cubits wide — matching the west end. The gate will occupy 20 of these 50 cubits (Exodus 27:16), with 15-cubit screens on each side. The eastern orientation of the entrance mirrors Eden (Genesis 3:24) and Ezekiel's vision (Ezekiel 43:1-4) — in Israelite sacred architecture, one enters from the east and moves westward toward the divine presence.
14: Fifteen cubits of linen hanging on one side of the gate, with three pillars and three sockets. The gate flanks create a framing for the entrance — the 20-cubit gate is proportionally centered within the 50-cubit east end, with 15 cubits of screening on each side. Approach to the divine dwelling is architecturally managed: the gate is wide (20 cubits) and well-lit (open to the east), but it is the only entry point.
15: Fifteen cubits on the other side of the gate — three pillars and three sockets. The symmetrical flanking of the entrance creates a formal gateway. The tabernacle has one gate facing east — one entrance to the sacred precincts. Jesus applies this spatial theology in John 10:9: 'I am the gate; whoever enters through me will be saved.' The exclusive entrance of the divine dwelling becomes a Christological claim.
16: masak sha'ar hechatzar (mah-SAK sha-AR heh-khatz-AR): 'screen/curtain for the gate of the courtyard' (H4539 H8179 H2691). The gate screen is 20 cubits wide, in the same four colors as the tabernacle entrance (tekhelet/argaman/shani/shesh) but with ma'aseh roqem (embroiderer's work H4639 H7551) rather than the most skilled weaving. Four pillars and four sockets for the 20-cubit gate. The outer gate is decorated but less elaborately than the inner — holiness increases as one moves inward.
17: kol ammudei hechatzar saviv meshhuqqim kesef: 'all the pillars of the courtyard all around with silver bands' — meshhuqqim (H2838 — banded/joined with silver). The courtyard pillars, regardless of which side, all share silver connecting rods and hooks. Silver unifies the entire courtyard at the top; bronze grounds every pillar at the bottom. The courtyard is literally ringed with silver — the redemption metal (cf. Ex Exodus 30:11-16 — silver as the ransom price of the census).
18: The full dimensions of the courtyard: orech hechatzar meah ba'amma uerochav chamishim bachameshin — length 100 cubits, width 50 cubits, height 5 cubits of twisted linen hangings. The courtyard height (5 cubits, ca. 2.3m) creates a privacy/separation zone without being so tall as to be oppressive — enough to mark off the sacred from the common while allowing air and light. The bronze sockets run beneath the entire perimeter.
19: kol kelei ha-mishkan bechol avodato: 'all the vessels/equipment of the tabernacle for all its service' — including all yetedotav (ye-teh-do-TAV): 'its tent pegs/stakes' (H3489). Even the tent pegs — the simplest, most utilitarian objects — are listed among the sacred equipment. Every peg that anchors the tabernacle to the Sinai desert is nechoshet (bronze), part of the consecrated complex. Nothing about the tabernacle is secular, not even the hardware buried in the ground.
20: shemen zayit zakh katit lammaor (sheh-MEN zay-IT zakh kah-TEET lah-mah-OR): 'pure beaten olive oil for the light' (H8081 H2132 H2134 H3795 H3974). Katit (H3795) means beaten/crushed — the oil is produced by pounding the olives, not pressing them under a stone. The finest grade of oil, from gently beaten olives. The word for olive press is gat shemen — precisely Gethsemane. The oil that feeds the eternal lamp is katit, crushed oil, from the garden whose name means crushing. ner tamid (NER tah-MEED): 'perpetual/continual lamp' (H5216 H8548). The lamp must not go out — it burns through the night before YHWH, the undying light of the covenant.
21: me'erev ad boqer (me-EH-rev ad BO-ker): 'from evening until morning' — the lamp burns through the night, the hours when darkness rules outside. Aaron and his sons tend the ner tamid in the tent of meeting before YHWH. chukkat olam ledorotam (khuk-KAT oh-LAM le-doh-roh-TAM): 'a perpetual statute throughout their generations' (H2708 H5769). The lampstand service is not temporary legislation but olam — the same word for the everlasting covenant. me'et benei Yisrael: the oil comes from Israel, supplied by the people, tended by the priests — the whole nation sustains the light that burns before the divine presence on their behalf.
